data.title: Swiss parliament topples proposed bill to limit executive bonuses in financial sector - report
data.description:
Switzerland's lawmakers have reportedly toppled a proposed bill that aimed to limit the bonus-driven incentive system in the country's financial industry.

The bill could have targeted variable pay for senior executives at banks such as UBS Group (UBS) and Raiffeisen (RAIFF) (RAIFY), Bloomberg News reported.

Swiss Parliament's upper house voted against the bill with a 32 to 9 majority.

High bonuses for CEOs are said to have come under fire recently in the country. Swiss politician, who is serving as head of the federal department of finance, Karin Keller-Sutter had reportedly said in 2024 that she would have to work for 30 years to earn the equivalent of UBS CEO Sergio Ermotti's pay.

Today, Keller-Sutter said before the Bern-based parliament that the government favors bonus clawbacks over management failures at large lenders. However, a ban on variable pay is not considered appropriate, according to the March 12 report.

data.title: U.S. stocks sink as war against Iran intensifies, oil prices keep climbing
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages were lower on Thursday as oil prices continued to climb due to the escalating U.S.-Israel-Iran conflict.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last -1.1%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1.4%, and the Dow Jones (DJI) was -1.2%.

Over in the bond market, the benchmark 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was 2 basis points higher at 4.25%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 6 basis points to 3.72%.

Crude oil (CL1:COM) rose 8.8% to $94 per barrel on Thursday, while Brent (CO1:COM) was also higher at $99.

“I find it hard not to have a central scenario where oil prices will remain very high for a long time, higher than the market current prices,” said Olivier Blanchard, Robert Solow Professor of economics emeritus at MIT.

“There is no reason, whether or not Trump declares that war is over, to think that Iran will not continue for some time to threaten to destroy the ships that try. Why should they stop? The risk will thus remain sufficiently high that most non-Iranian ships will not take the risk. Thus, the shortfall of 20 million barrels a day is likely to last for long,” he added.

The conflict in the Middle East has triggered one of the largest supply shocks of global oil markets in history, leading to disruption of almost 7.5% of the worldwide supply, according to the International Energy Agency.

In economic news, the initial jobless claims barely budged in the past week. The U.S. trade deficit narrowed more than expected in January.

Also, housing starts unexpectedly rose in January, while building permits dropped more than consensus.

The Federal Reserve balance sheet will be released later in the day.

data.title: MariMed outlines 2026 growth strategy as wholesale revenue rises 11% and new state expansions advance
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MariMed Inc. (MRMD) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jon Levine, CEO, highlighted a sixth consecutive year of positive adjusted EBITDA and emphasized the company's 1% revenue growth in 2025, stating "2025 also marked the sixth consecutive year we generated positive adjusted EBITDA." Levine outlined three strategic pillars: capturing meaningful market share in existing markets, expanding brands into new markets, and strengthening the balance sheet. He reported, "Betty's Eddies was the #1 selling edible across the markets where it's available," and described Vibations as ranking fourth in the beverage category. Levine noted expansion efforts into Pennsylvania and New York, and a licensing launch for Betty's Eddies in Maine. The company restructured its Series B preferred shares, extending maturity and enhancing financial flexibility. Levine described retail as "the majority of our revenue and operating cash flow" and announced plans for a new Thrive store in Ohio.
  • Ryan Crandall, Chief Commercial Officer, reported that aggregate wholesale revenue increased 11% in 2025, now representing 44% of total revenue. He stated, "We increased our penetration into dispensaries in 2025 by 200 basis points, selling our brands into 85% of retail stores in the markets in which we operate." Crandall described sequential retail growth of 4% in Q4 and highlighted the success of initiatives such as centralizing retail buying, improving assortments, and expanding the loyalty program, which saw a 31% year-over-year membership increase.
  • Mario Pinho, CFO, reported, "For the fourth quarter, total revenue was $41.7 million, bringing full year 2025 revenue to $159.8 million, representing 1.3% sequential growth in the quarter and 7% growth for the full year." Pinho detailed that retail revenue for the quarter was $23.4 million, up 3.6% sequentially, and wholesale revenue was $17.6 million. He noted, "We ended the year with $8.9 million in cash and cash equivalents, up from $7.3 million at the end of 2024."

Outlook

  • Levine described the primary 2026 growth drivers as continued wholesale penetration, full-year contributions from Delaware's expanding adult-use market, the Maine licensing partnership, and anticipated revenue from the new Ohio dispensary. He added, "We will do everything in our control to move up the time line for distribution of our brands in Pennsylvania and New York."
  • Management indicated that growth strategies do not depend on federal reforms but rather on disciplined capital allocation and execution.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter total revenue was $41.7 million, with full-year revenue at $159.8 million, reflecting 1.3% sequential quarterly growth and 7% full-year growth.
  • Retail revenue for the quarter was $23.4 million, up 3.6% sequentially. Wholesale revenue for the quarter was $17.6 million.
  • Non-GAAP cost of revenue for the quarter was $25 million, with gross profit of $16.5 million and a non-GAAP gross margin of 40%.
  • Total operating expenses for the year were $56.9 million, a 0.7% increase compared to 2024. Operating income for the quarter was $2.4 million and $8.8 million for the year.
  • Non-GAAP EBITDA for the year was $16.9 million, with a margin of 10.5%.
  • Cash and cash equivalents were $8.9 million at year-end.

Q&A

  • Pablo Zuanic, Zuanic & Associates, questioned the stabilization of Illinois retail revenues and future deflation. Ryan Crandall responded, "I think we still see some price compression happening in Illinois. But I do tend to agree with you overall that we do believe the market seems to be stabilizing, at least our stores seem to be stabilizing. But price compression is still real in that market, and it is forecasted to compress more in '26."
  • Zuanic inquired about total Illinois and Massachusetts revenues. Mario Pinho replied, "Sequentially in Illinois, we were down and primarily driven by the retail side of our product revenue."
  • Zuanic asked about Delaware's store growth outlook. Crandall answered, "We have seen some new stores getting close to coming online. But at this point, it has been a relatively slow rollout of new stores."
  • Zuanic asked about the Ohio store opening and sustainability of Maryland store growth. Levine stated, "The Ohio store, I can't put an exact time line on it, but we do know that it will be in this year." On Maryland, "that business has been very strong... I do believe that it will continue to see additional growth."
  • Zuanic asked about M&A and asset-light strategy. Levine confirmed, "The big growth opportunities is to still be active in the M&A and we're out there talking to quite a few different groups."
  • Joseph Gomes, NOBLE Capital, clarified anticipated contributions from Pennsylvania and New York. Levine responded, "We're hopeful that we can get that operation up and running before the end of the year and build the inventory to start revenue either late the year or early in '27."
  • Gomes asked about additional penetration opportunities. Crandall said, "the ability to go wider and deeper with these customers ... that's our opportunity."
  • Gomes queried interest expense from the refi. Levine answered, "That number is approximately $800,000."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed neutral to slightly positive sentiment, with questions focused on stabilization, expansion timing, and pricing pressure, but also recognizing brand strength and market positioning.
  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing brand leadership, disciplined strategy, and financial flexibility. Levine stated, "we are confident in MariMed's tremendous upside over the long term."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident but more focused on operational discipline and capital allocation, while analysts continued to probe on price compression and expansion timelines.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter's guidance language reflects a continued emphasis on disciplined capital allocation and expansion via both licensing and M&A, similar to the previous quarter, but with greater focus on balance sheet strength and asset-light expansion.
  • Strategic focus shifted more toward wholesale growth, licensing, and leveraging brand leadership in core and new markets, particularly in Delaware, Maine, Pennsylvania, and New York.
  • Analysts maintained a focus on pricing pressure, stabilization in key states, and clarity on expansion timing, echoing previous quarter concerns.
  • Key metric changes included an 11% increase in wholesale revenue and increased retail penetration, with continued margin discipline and cash preservation.
  • Management’s confidence in brand strength and operational execution remained high, as did analysts' cautious inquiries about pricing and market dynamics.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing price compression in mature markets, particularly Illinois, and forecasted further compression in 2026.
  • Slow rollout of new stores in Delaware was noted as a challenge, though the company is preparing to meet demand as stores open.
  • Expansion into Pennsylvania and New York is dependent on state approvals and construction timelines, introducing uncertainty in the timing of new revenue streams.
  • The company highlighted a disciplined M&A approach, avoiding deals that could weaken the balance sheet amid industry-wide "fire sales."

Final Takeaway

MariMed closed 2025 with modest revenue growth, strengthened market positions in core states, and expanded into new markets through licensing and managed services. The company’s disciplined approach to capital allocation, margin preservation, and balance sheet restructuring, combined with strong wholesale performance and brand leadership, positions it for continued progress in 2026. Strategic priorities include expanding market share, accelerating brand distribution in new states, and leveraging both organic and M&A-driven growth opportunities, all while navigating persistent pricing pressures and operational challenges in key markets.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bitcoin price faces pressure as crude oil rally tops $100 mark
data.description:
As crude oil prices rise, risk assets are starting to weaken. Bitcoin (BTC-USD) and the S&P 500 are showing pressure amid the Iran war conflict and increasing inflation concerns, while energy markets continue to gain strength.

According to TradingView data, WTI crude oil is trading around $96 per barrel on the 2-hour chart at the time of writing. The chart shows that it moved in a steady uptrend earlier, slowly rising from about $70 levels while making higher highs and higher lows.  

As the trend continued, crude oil moved sharply higher, crossing the $100 mark and reaching a recent weekly high of around $119 per barrel.

But at the same time, Bitcoin (BTC-USD), along with the S&P 500, shows slight weakness. On the 1-hour TradingView chart, BTC is trading near $70k at the time of writing. However, over the past week, it has declined by around 3%. 

Similarly, the S&P 500 is currently trading around 6,715. Over the past five days, the index has fallen by about 53 points, which is a 0.8% decline. 

Looking at the present scenario, a short-term correlation can be seen between these markets. During the week, energy prices have moved higher, increasing inflation worries, which made investors more cautious about riskier assets like stocks and cryptocurrencies.

Levels investors should watch:

  • Crude Oil Levels: Support sits near $92 and resistance near $100. A break above $100 could restart the bullish trend and push prices toward the $120 zone.
  • Bitcoin (BTC-USD) Levels: Support sits near $69K and resistance near $71K. If oil breaks resistance, the asset may face short-term pressure. For now, the RSI near 50 shows neutral momentum.

In the near term, the movement in the energy market could remain an important signal for the broader financial industry.

data.title: Citi Research puts Delta and SkyWest on 30-day positive catalyst watch
data.description:
,With oil prices continuing to inch higher and increased fuel costs weighing on the airline sector, Citi Research highlights two carriers it believes are largely insulated from the worst of the impact. 

Analyst John Godyn puts Delta Air Lines (DAL) and SkyWest (SKYW) on a positive 30-day catalyst watch while maintaining a Buy rating on Delta (DAL) and Neutral for SkyWest (SKYW).

“The spike in fuel is extremely likely to weigh on all airline earnings in the short-term regardless of mitigating factors,” Godyn says in his note to clients. However, both Delta (DAL) and SkyWest (SKYW) have the least sensitivity to shocks.

Thanks to its “Trainer Refinery” asset, 75% of Delta’s (DAL) fuel consumption is covered by this Pennsylvania petroleum facility that is owned through Delta’s (DAL) Monroe Energy refinery. The carrier acquired the facility in 2012 for $180M to stabilize jet fuel costs and secure an uninterrupted supply for its northeastern U.S. hubs, including JFK International and LaGuardia.

Delta (DAL) also enjoys the highest pre-tax profit margin in the airline industry, which serves as a buffer to EPS sensitivity and generates high-teens percentage of revenue from its Atlantic routes – second only to United (UAL).

SkyWest (SKYW) also enjoys a high pre-tax margin that reduces earnings volatility amid periods of economic shocks. But SkyWest (SKYW) also enjoys a unique regional airline contract flying business model where fuel is a complete pass-through (~90% of total capacity). And without any financing obligations on its fleet, the carrier can absorb any necessary capacity reductions.

data.title: The stock market is ‘overvalued’ and a correction is due – Omega Family Office CEO
data.description:
Leon Cooperman, chairman and CEO of Omega Family Office, warned that financial markets are dangerously overpriced as stocks have become too costly relative to company profits, signaling that a market correction is due.

“The stock market is not discounting the uncertainty of the environment in a proper manner,” Cooperman said.

The former hedge fund manager, who converted his firm into a family office in 2018 and is worth $3.8B, pointed to the possible threat of stagflation or a recession as key concerns.

Cooperman attributed the market’s overvaluation to mounting geopolitical tensions and dysfunction in Washington.

“The market doesn’t deserve to be where it’s selling,” he said, adding that “it’s too expensive.”

The S&P 500 (SP500) has traded as high as 23x the next 12 months’ estimated earnings in recent months, marking the benchmark’s richest valuation since 2020, according to market data.

Economists surveyed by Bloomberg assign a 25% probability of recession, while Mohamed El-Erian, the former chief executive officer of Pacific Investment Management Co., has warned of a “new stagflationary wind” starting to blow through the global economy.

Natixis economists have outlined scenarios in which U.S. growth slows to as little as 0.5% this year, or in the worst-case, contracts for several quarters if the conflict in Iran broadens.

Market volatility has also emerged as a significant worry for Cooperman, with swings reaching their highest level since April 2025’s tariff shock amid the U.S. and Israel’s war with Iran and oil price swings.

The S&P 500 (SP500) saw intraday swings of at least 1% on more than half of this year’s trading days, the billionaire CEO noted.

“Good news is treated with a yawn and bad news basically sends a stock down 15% or 20% on average,” he said, highlighting the asymmetric reaction investors are showing to market developments.

S&P ETFs: (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), (SPXU), (FXAIX), (VFIAX), (VFFSX), and (SWPPX).

data.title: Rivian unveils the new R2 electric SUV as it looks to scale up
data.description:
Rivian Automotive (RIVN) formally introduced its R2 mid-size electric SUV on Thursday.

The electric vehicle maker said R2 deliveries will start this spring, beginning with the R2 Performance with Launch Package (starting at $57,990), followed by additional premium configurations in late 2026 and standard configurations in 2027 with a starting price of $46,495. The R2 lineup will offer up to 656 horsepower, 0–60 mph in as quick as 3.6 seconds, while offering a Rivian-estimated range of up to 345 miles. Standard features will include a native NACS charging port and Autonomy+ hardware.

"R2 embodies so many of our learnings that we have accumulated—the passion and attention to detail from our team is visible everywhere," highlighted Rivian (RIVN) CEO RJ Scaringe.

Benchmark Capital sees the R2 launch as a positive for Rivian (RIVN). "We view the event as an important demand signal and proof point for manufacturing execution," updated analyst Mickey Legg. 

In Car and Driver's review of the R2, the model was noted to have more rear-seat space than rivals such as the Ford (F) Mustang Mach-E and the Tesla (TSLA) Model Y. The model was also said to have reasonable off-roading abilities.

Rivian (RIVN) plans to show off the model at the SXSW festival in Austin, Texas, today.

Shares of Rivian (RIVN) were down 3.5% in late morning trading.

data.title: RWE plans $20B push for its first U.S. gas plants to meet data center demand
data.description:
RWE (RWEOY) +3.8% in European trading Thursday after saying it expects to invest around half of its €35B ($40.4B) spending plan over the next five years on U.S. power assets, including new gas-fired power plants, as it seeks to capitalize on growing energy demand from data centers.

The German power producer said it will focus on 15 gas generation projects in Texas, Arizona and parts of the Midwest, while also further expanding its portfolio of wind and solar power, as well as battery storage facilities, according to its full-year results.

"The USA remains our most important growth market, not least due to the rapidly growing demand for electricity," RWE (RWEOY) CEO Markus Krebber said. "We are further strengthening our position with the addition of flexible gas-fired power plants."

RWE (RWEOY) also said it plans to spend €9B by 2031 in gas plants and batteries in its home market Germany, as well as €7B in onshore and solar in European core markets and Australia, plus €2B in offshore wind.

The company said it expects the spending plan to drive average adjusted earnings per share growth of 12% annually over the next five years, with adjusted earnings rising to €4.40 in 2031 from €2.48 in 2025, and translating into 10% annual increases in its dividend, which was €1.20 for 2025.

U.S. spending would take its installed capacity in the country to 22 GW by 2031 from 13 GW currently, the company said.

For 2025, RWE (RWEOY) said ⁠full-year results showed core profit fell 10% to €5.1B but still topped the €4.9B analyst consensus in a company-provided poll.

data.title: AI, chip stocks tumble after Iran war causes 'largest supply disruption' in global oil market
data.description:
Chip and AI-related stocks were largely in the red on Thursday as oil prices continued to surge amid the escalating U.S.-Israel-Iran war nearing two weeks.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) fell around 1.4%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) declined about 1.2%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) fell nearly 1.3%.

On Thursday, the International Energy Agency said that the war in the Middle East is creating the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market.

Brent Futures (CO1:COM) jumped about 9%, while Crude Oil Futures (CL1:COM) surged nearly 9.6% on Thursday. The U.S. Navy is “not ready” to escort oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz, but “quite likely” will begin to do so by the end of the month, Secretary of Energy Chris Wright said Thursday.

Global markets are “struggling to price where the risks are” amid the ongoing war, according to Nick Nelson, head of global equity strategy at Absolute Strategy Research.

Shares of AI chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA) fell about 2%, while Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) slumped nearly 3%. Broadcom (AVGO) declined roughly 2%, while Qualcomm (QCOM) dipped about 1%.

Several other AI and networking-related stocks were largely in the red on Thursday. Lumentum (LITE) tumbled around 6%, while Coherent (COHR) slumped nearly 3%. Ciena (CIEN), Celestica (CLS), and Arista Networks (ANET) each fell about 2%. Cisco (CSCO) dipped roughly 1%.

Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC) and GlobalFoundries (GFS) each slumped about 6%, while Micron Technology (MU) and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) each tumbled around 5%. Arm (ARM), Analog Devices (ADI), and Intel (INTC) each fell nearly 4%. Texas Instruments (TXN) declined about 3%, while Marvell Technology (MRVL) fell nearly 2%.

 Chip equipment makers: Lam Research (LRCX) tumbled around 5%, while Applied Materials (AMAT) and KLA (KLAC) each slumped about 4%. ASML (ASML) fell nearly 3%.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Microsoft paves path to 'medical superintelligence' with Copilot Health
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) is expanding its AI-powered consumer health offerings with the introduction of Copilot Health, which draws data from an array of sources, including wearable devices, health records, and lab test results, to provide what it calls medical intelligence.

It believes this new feature will help pave the way for "medical superintelligence," which Microsoft defines as "health AI that can ultimately combine the wide-ranging knowledge of a general physician with the depth of a specialist."

Copilot Health will be made available through a gradual rollout process. It will initially launch in English in the U.S. for those 18 and older. Those interested in early access can join a waitlist.

It gathers data such as activity levels, sleep patterns, and vital signs from more than 50 wearable devices such as Fitbit, Oura, and Apple (AAPL) Health. It can also incorporate health records, such as visit summaries, medication lists, and test results, from more than 50,000 U.S. hospitals and provider organizations through HealthEx. Copilot Health can pull in lab results from Function as well.

Microsoft said Copilot Health conversations and data are isolated from general Copilot and will not be used for training AI models. It has stricter access, privacy, and safety controls, including encryption, and gives users the ability to manage and delete data. 

Although Apple has been at the forefront of collecting health data and providing insights for consumers, many others have entered the field. Early this year, OpenAI (OPENAI) released ChatGPT Health, which was aimed at helping users "feel more informed, prepared, and confident."

Earlier this month, Amazon (AMZN) launched its health AI agent on its website and app. The agentic AI solution is designed to provide personalized health information and guidance to patients based on lab results and other medical records accessed through the Health Information Exchange with consent.
